en.extract:
The azure-hooded jay (Cyanolyca cucullata) is a species of bird in the family Corvidae. It is found in Middle America. Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist montane forest. This species is known to have four subspecies. It is 11 to 12 inches (28 to 30 cm) in length and is dark blue with a black head and upper chest. The back of the head and neck are sky blue with a white border.
The jays travel in groups of two to ten individuals and may join mixed-species flocks. It is a secretive species and therefore difficult to observe in the wild. As an omnivore, this jay eats berries, seeds, and small, dead animals. Females lay three to four eggs, and the young fledge after twenty days. This species is listed as Least Concern, meaning it is not threatened with extinction.


Taxonomy
The species was first described by the American ornithologist Robert Ridgway in 1885. Its specific epithet, cucullata, is the Latin word for "hooded". Its closest relative is the beautiful jay (C. pulchra) of Colombia and Ecuador; in his 1934 study, Hellmayr treated these species as conspecific. Phylogenetic analysis published in 2009 confirmed the close relationship between the two species; Bonaccorso speculates that the geographic (and subsequent genetic) separation between these species and others in the genus Cyanolyca may have been initiated by the formation of the Río Cauca Valley in western Colombia.
The azure-hooded jay has four subspecies. Cyanolyca cucullata mitrata is found in eastern Mexico, from San Luis Potosí to north central Oaxaca. This subspecies was initially treated as a separate species by Ridgway, but it was later merged into the azure-hooded jay. C. c. guatemalae ranges from southern Mexico in Chiapas to central Guatemala. C. c. hondurensis resides in western Honduras. C. c. cucullata, the nominate subspecies, is found in Costa Rica and western Panama.


Description

The azure-hooded jay ranges in length from 11 to 12 inches (28 to 30 cm), and it tends to weigh 3.52 ounces (100 g). Its large size and frame help the bird manage the large amount of flying it does. The adult is dark blue with black on the head and upper chest, while the rear of the crown and nape, or back of the neck, are sky blue with a white border. The legs and bill are black and the eyes are dark red. Both sexes are similar in appearance. Juveniles are duller than adults and their sky blue hood does not possess the white bordering.
Its voice has been described as a loud and bright eihnk-eihnk that is typically repeated four to five times. It is also known to repeat a nasal ehr-ehn or eh'enk noise twice and give off a low, gruff, hard cheh-r. The alarm and flock-social calls of this species, characterized as a reek! sound, are "nasal, querulous, and upwardly or double inflected."


Distribution and habitat

This species is known from Costa Rica, Guatemala, Honduras, southeastern Mexico, and western Panama. It lives in humid evergreen forests that are sometimes interspersed with pine trees. It can be found at the edges of these forests typically in the middle and higher levels within these trees. It is also normally found only where cloud forest is uninterrupted.


Ecology and behavior
This corvid is known to join mixed-species flocks with other species including unicolored jays and emerald toucanets. It is also known to travel in groups with two to ten other azure-hooded jays. It is a skulking and secretive species, rarely coming out into the open. Because of this habit, the bird is extremely difficult to observe in the wild and not much is known about its ecology. Mates are known to preen each other, a process which entails one bird bending over in front of the other and tugging on its throat feathers. The feathers of the crown are often moving swiftly, and it is believed that the condition of a mate can be determined by this movement.
Like other jays, this species is likely extremely intelligent. Similar species are known to use ants to keep their feathers clean, store seeds and nuts for later consumption, and use their toes to hold food. However, due to its secretive nature, these characteristics have not yet been observed in the species.
The bird's bright plumage makes it easy for predators to find this species. Whenever the jay feels threatened, it gives off a warning alarm call.


Diet
The azure-hooded jay is omnivorous, eating berries, seeds, and small, dead animals. This species has been known to steal and eat bait from traps set for small mammals. The bird tends to forage in the forest canopy.


Reproduction
The jay's nest is typically built 16.4 to 23 feet (5 to 7 m) above the ground next to a tree trunk. The base of the azure-hooded jay's first studied nest was coarsely made out of twigs that were 0.08 to 0.12 inches (2 to 3 mm) long. That nest was about 4.3 inches (11 cm) wide inside and 7.4 to 13 inches (19 to 33 cm) wide overall depending on the length of the exterior twigs. The nest is 2 inches (5 cm) deep and has an interior constructed with woven thin fibrils and twigs, and no feathers or other softening devices are used in the nest's construction. In addition to building its own nest, this jay is known to reuse old, abandoned nests made by other species. Three to four eggs are normally laid. The young are typically raised in the nest between April and June and they take at least 20 days to fledge. Both parents care for the young and feed them a variety of insects, including katydids. After the young fledge, they stay close to their parents.


Conservation
This jay is treated as a species of Least Concern, or not threatened with extinction, by BirdLife International due to its large geographical range of about 42,500 square miles (110,000 km2), population which, while unsurveyed, is believed to be above 10,000 individuals, and lack of a 30% population decline over the last ten years. However, the azure-hooded jay is uncommon in some parts of its range. It is also believed that deforestation may have an effect on this bird.


Relationship with humans
Although this species has not been observed doing this, closely related jays are known to destroy and eat human-planted crops such as orchards, cane, pineapples, and potatoes. The azure-hooded jay has appeared on one stamp in Mexico in 1996.


References


Cited texts
Goodwin D. (1983). Crows of the World. Queensland University Press, St Lucia, Qld. ISBN 0-7022-1015-3.


External links
Azure-hooded jay sound recordings
BirdLife Species Factsheet

The '''azure-hooded jay''' ('''''Cyanolyca cucullata''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Corvidae]]. It is found in [[Middle America (Americas)|Middle America]]. Its natural [[habitat]] is [[subtropical]] or [[tropical]] moist [[montane forest]]. This species is known to have four [[subspecies]]. It is {{convert|11|to|12|in|cm}} in length and is dark blue with a black head and upper chest. The back of the head and neck are sky blue with a white border.

The jays travel in groups of two to ten individuals and may join [[mixed-species flock]]s. It is a secretive species and therefore difficult to observe in the wild. As an [[omnivore]], this jay eats berries, seeds, and small, [[carrion|dead animals]]. Females lay three to four eggs, and the young [[fledge]] after twenty days. This species is listed as [[Least Concern]], meaning it is not threatened with extinction.

==Taxonomy==
The species was first described by the American ornithologist [[Robert Ridgway]] in 1885. Its [[specific name (zoology)|specific epithet]], ''cucullata'', is the [[Latin]] word for "hooded". Its closest relative is the [[beautiful jay]] (''C. pulchra'') of Colombia and Ecuador;<ref>Goodwin, p. 258</ref> in his 1934 study, Hellmayr treated these species as [[conspecific]].<ref>{{cite book |title=Catalogue of the Birds of the Americas and the Adjacent Islands |url=https://archive.org/details/catalogueofbirds138hell |last=Hellmayr |first=Carl Eduard |year=1934 |publisher=Field Museum of Natural History |location=Chicago}}</ref> [[Phylogenetic]] analysis published in 2009 confirmed the close relationship between the two species; Bonaccorso speculates that the geographic (and subsequent genetic) separation between these species and others in the genus ''[[Cyanolyca]]'' may have been initiated by the formation of the [[Cauca River|Río Cauca Valley]] in western Colombia.<ref name="pmid19135159">{{cite journal |author=Bonaccorso E |title=Historical biogeography and speciation in the neotropical highlands: molecular phylogenetics of the jay genus ''Cyanolyca'' |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=50 |issue=3 |pages=618–632 |date=March 2009 |pmid=19135159 |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2008.12.012 }}</ref>

The azure-hooded jay has four subspecies.<ref name=" Clements ">{{cite book| last = Clements| first = James F| author-link = James Clements (ornithologist) | title = The Clements Checklist of Birds of the World Sixth Edition| publisher = Comstock Publishing Associates| year = 2007| location = Ithaca, NY| page= 589| isbn = 978-0-8014-4501-9| title-link = The Clements Checklist of Birds of the World}}</ref> ''Cyanolyca cucullata mitrata'' is found in eastern Mexico, from [[San Luis Potosí]] to north central [[Oaxaca]]. This subspecies was initially treated as a separate species by Ridgway, but it was later merged into the azure-hooded jay.<ref name=" Ridgway "/> ''C. c. guatemalae'' ranges from southern Mexico in [[Chiapas]] to central Guatemala.<ref name=" Clements"/> ''C. c. hondurensis'' resides in western Honduras.<ref name=" Clements"/> ''C. c. cucullata'', the [[Nominate subspecies#Nominate subspecies|nominate subspecies]], is found in Costa Rica and western Panama.<ref name=" Clements"/>

==Description==
[[File:Cyanolyca cucullata perched.jpg|thumb|left|In Costa Rica]]
The azure-hooded jay ranges in length from {{convert|11|to|12|in|cm}},<ref name=" Howell ">{{cite book | last = Howell | first = Steve N.G. | author2 = Sophie Webb | title = A Guide to the Birds of Mexico and Northern Central America | publisher = Oxford University Press | year = 1995 | location = New York | page = [https://archive.org/details/guidetobirdsofme0000howe/page/542 542] | isbn = 0-19-854012-4 | title-link = A Guide to the Birds of Mexico and Northern Central America }}</ref> and it tends to weigh {{convert|3.52|oz|g}}.<ref name=" Kieltyka"/> Its large size and frame help the bird manage the large amount of flying it does.<ref name=" Kieltyka"/> The adult is dark blue with black on the head and upper chest, while the rear of the [[crown (anatomy)|crown]] and [[nape]], or back of the neck, are sky blue with a white border.<ref name=" Howell "/> The legs and [[beak|bill]] are black and the eyes are dark red.<ref name=" Howell "/><ref name= "DTW">{{cite web| title = Azure-hooded Jay| publisher = Discovery Travel World | year = 2009 | url = http://www.1-costaricalink.com/costa_rica_fauna/azure_hooded_jay.htm | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20071015214448/http://www.1-costaricalink.com/costa_rica_fauna/azure_hooded_jay.htm | url-status = usurped | archive-date = October 15, 2007 | access-date =2009-04-17 }}</ref> Both sexes are similar in appearance.<ref name=" Ridgway">{{cite book | last = Ridgway | first = Robert | author-link = Robert Ridgway | title = The Birds of North and Middle America: A Descriptive Catalogue of the Higher Groups' Genera, Species, and Subspecies of Birds Known to Occur in North America, from the Arctic Lands to the Isthmus of Panama, the West Indies and Other Islands of the Caribbean Sea, and the Galapagos Archipelago Part III | publisher = United States National Museum | year = 1904 | location = Washington | page = 322 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=TagKAAAAIAAJ&q=%22Azure-hooded+Jay%22&pg=PA322}}</ref> Juveniles are duller than adults and their sky blue hood does not possess the white bordering.<ref name=" Howell "/>

Its voice has been described as a loud and bright ''eihnk-eihnk'' that is typically repeated four to five times. It is also known to repeat a nasal ''ehr-ehn'' or ''eh'enk'' noise twice and give off a low, gruff, hard ''cheh-r''.<ref name=" Howell "/> The alarm and flock-social calls of this species, characterized as a ''reek!'' sound, are "nasal, querulous, and upwardly or double inflected."<ref>{{cite journal |last=Hardy |first=John William |year=1967 |title=The puzzling vocal repertoire of the South American Collared Jay, ''Cyanolyca viridicyana merida'' |journal=The Condor |volume=69 |issue=5 |pages=513–521 |doi=10.2307/1366150 |jstor=1366150 |publisher=Cooper Ornithological Society}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:Monte verde.jpg|right|thumb|Costa Rica's [[Monteverde]] cloud forest, which the azure-hooded jay inhabits]]
This species is known from [[Costa Rica]], [[Guatemala]], [[Honduras]], southeastern [[Mexico]], and western [[Panama]].<ref name=" Ridgely">{{Cite book| last1 = Ridgely| first1 = Robert S| last2 = Gwynne Jr.| first2 = John A.| title = A Guide to the Birds of Panama with Costa Rica, Nicaragua, and Honduras| place= Princeton, NJ| publisher = Princeton University Press| year =1989| edition = Second| pages = 337–338| isbn =0-691-02512-6 }}</ref> It lives in humid evergreen forests that are sometimes interspersed with pine trees.<ref name=" Howell "/><ref name=" Kieltyka"/> It can be found at the edges of these forests typically in the middle and higher levels within these trees.<ref name="DTW"/><ref name=" Ridgely "/><ref name="Winnett-Murray">{{cite journal |author1=Winnett-Murray, Kathy |author2=Murray, K. Greg |author3=Busby, William  |name-list-style=amp | title = Two nests of the Azure-hooded Jay with notes on nest attendance | journal = The Wilson Bulletin | volume = 100 | issue = 1| pages = 134–135| date = March 1988| url = http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v100n01/p0134-p0135.pdf}}</ref> It is also normally found only where [[cloud forest]] is uninterrupted.<ref name=" Kieltyka"/>

==Ecology and behavior==
This corvid is known to join [[mixed-species flock]]s with other species including [[unicolored jay]]s and [[emerald toucanet]]s.<ref name=" Howell "/> It is also known to travel in groups with two to ten other azure-hooded jays.<ref name=" Kieltyka"/><ref name=" Ridgely "/> It is a skulking and secretive species, rarely coming out into the open.<ref name=" Howell "/> Because of this habit, the bird is extremely difficult to observe in the wild and not much is known about its [[ecology]].<ref name=" Kieltyka"/> Mates are known to preen each other, a process which entails one bird bending over in front of the other and tugging on its throat feathers.<ref name=" Kieltyka"/> The feathers of the [[crown (anatomy)|crown]] are often moving swiftly, and it is believed that the condition of a mate can be determined by this movement.<ref name=" Kieltyka"/>

Like other jays, this species is likely extremely intelligent. Similar species are known to use ants to keep their feathers clean, store seeds and nuts for later consumption, and use their toes to hold food. However, due to its secretive nature, these characteristics have not yet been observed in the species.<ref name=" Kieltyka"/>

The bird's bright [[plumage]] makes it easy for predators to find this species.<ref name=" Kieltyka"/> Whenever the jay feels threatened, it gives off a warning alarm call.<ref name=" Kieltyka"/>

===Diet===
The azure-hooded jay is [[Omnivore|omnivorous]], eating berries, seeds, and small, [[carrion|dead animals]].<ref name=" Kieltyka"/> This species has been known to steal and eat bait from traps set for small mammals.<ref name=" Kieltyka"/> The bird tends to forage in the [[forest canopy]].<ref name=" Kieltyka"/>

===Reproduction===
The jay's nest is typically built {{convert|16.4|to|23|ft|m|0}} above the ground next to a tree trunk.<ref name="Winnett-Murray"/> The base of the azure-hooded jay's first studied nest was coarsely made out of twigs that were {{convert|0.08|to|0.12|in|mm|0}} long. That nest was about {{convert|4.3|in|cm|0}} wide inside and {{convert|7.4|to|13|in|cm}} wide overall depending on the length of the exterior twigs.<ref name="Winnett-Murray"/> The nest is {{convert|2|in|cm|0}} deep and has an interior constructed with woven thin fibrils and twigs, and no feathers or other softening devices are used in the nest's construction.<ref name="Winnett-Murray"/> In addition to building its own nest, this jay is known to reuse old, abandoned nests made by other species.<ref name=" Kieltyka"/> Three to four eggs are normally laid.<ref name=" Kieltyka"/> The young are typically raised in the nest between April and June and they take at least 20 days to [[fledge]]. Both parents care for the young and feed them a variety of insects, including [[Tettigoniidae|katydid]]s.<ref name="Winnett-Murray"/> After the young fledge, they stay close to their parents.<ref name="Winnett-Murray"/>

==Conservation==
This jay is treated as a species of [[Least Concern]], or not threatened with extinction, by [[BirdLife International]] due to its large geographical range of about {{convert|42500|mi2|km2}}, population which, while unsurveyed, is believed to be above 10,000 individuals, and lack of a 30% population decline over the last ten years.<ref name="BL">{{cite web| title =Species factsheet: ''Cyanolyca cucullata''| publisher =BirdLife International| year =2008| url =http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/index.html?action=SpcHTMDetails.asp&sid=5696&m=0| access-date =2009-04-16}}</ref> However, the azure-hooded jay is uncommon in some parts of its range.<ref name=" BL "/> It is also believed that [[deforestation]] may have an effect on this bird.<ref name=" Kieltyka ">{{cite web | last = Kieltyka | first = Zachary | title = ''Cyanolyca cucullata''| publisher = Animal Diversity Web | year = 2007 | url = http://animaldiversity.ummz.umich.edu/site/accounts/information/Cyanolyca_cucullata.html| access-date = 2009-04-19}}</ref>

==Relationship with humans==
Although this species has not been observed doing this, closely related jays are known to destroy and eat human-planted crops such as orchards, cane, pineapples, and potatoes.<ref name=" Kieltyka"/> The azure-hooded jay has appeared on one stamp in Mexico in 1996.<ref name="Stamp ">{{cite web | title =Azure-hooded Jay Stamps | publisher =Bird Stamps | url = http://www.bird-stamps.org/cspecies/18903000.htm| archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20010113195400/http://www.bird%2Dstamps.org/cspecies/18903000.htm| url-status = usurped| archive-date = January 13, 2001| access-date = 2009-04-16}}</ref>

zh.extract:
青蓝头鹊（学名：Cyanolyca cucullata）是鸦科蓝头鹊属的一种，分布于尼加拉瓜、危地马拉、哥斯达黎加、墨西哥、巴拿马和洪都拉斯。全球活动范围约为105,000平方千米。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
青蓝头鹊的平均体重约为101.6克。栖息地包括亚热带或热带的湿润低地林和亚热带或热带的湿润山地林。


参考文献

Cyanolyca cucullata. BirdLife.org.   [2011-06-27]. （原始内容存档于2016-03-04）.
